claim: Anthropic's official devcontainer ships an init-firewall.sh that sets iptables default-drop on INPUT/FORWARD/OUTPUT, allows DNS/SSH/loopback/established, then accepts egress only for an ipset 'allowed-domains' populated from registry.npmjs.org, api.anthropic.com, sentry.io, statsig.anthropic.com, marketplace.visualstudio.com, and GitHub CIDRs fetched from api.github.com/meta.
quote: iptables -P OUTPUT DROP ... iptables -A OUTPUT -m set --match-set allowed-domains dst -j ACCEPT ... iptables -A OUTPUT -j REJECT --reject-with icmp-admin-prohibited

claim: Claude Code's native /sandbox uses bubblewrap on Linux/WSL2 and Seatbelt on macOS for filesystem isolation, and routes egress through a proxy server that enforces a domain allowlist, with explicit warnings that allowing /var/run/docker.sock via allowUnixSockets grants full host access and that broad domains like github.com enable exfiltration.
quote: The `allowUnixSockets` configuration can inadvertently grant access to powerful system services ... if it is used to allow access to `/var/run/docker.sock` this would effectively grant access to the host system through exploiting the docker socket.

claim: Anthropic's engineering post states that effective sandboxing requires both filesystem and network isolation, because network-only isolation still lets an agent exfiltrate SSH keys and filesystem-only isolation still lets it reach exfil endpoints.
quote: effective sandboxing requires _both_ filesystem and network isolation. Without both: Network-only isolation allows exfiltration of sensitive files (SSH keys); Filesystem-only isolation permits escape and network access exploitation.

claim: Simon Willison's lethal-trifecta framing identifies the three conditions that make prompt-injection exfiltration possible — private-data access, untrusted content, and outbound comms — and argues sandboxing is the main lever to cut the trifecta by limiting what private data and exfil channels the agent has.
quote: The lethal trifecta for AI agents: access to your private data, exposure to untrusted content, and the ability to externally communicate in a way that could be used to steal your data (exfiltration).

claim: A widely-shared community reference implementation for Claude Code uses docker-compose with cap_add NET_ADMIN (only to let an entrypoint configure iptables at start), then drops to a non-root devuser, default-denies egress, and allowlists api.anthropic.com, claude.ai, registry.npmjs.org, and github.com.
quote: iptables -P INPUT DROP ... iptables -P OUTPUT DROP ... Claude installs as a non-root user ('devuser'), though the container entrypoint runs as root specifically for firewall initialization.

claim: The FoamoftheSea claude-code-sandbox layers defense-in-depth for --dangerously-skip-permissions mode: non-root devuser with sudo scoped to only lock-settings.sh and init-firewall.sh, root-owned read-only claude-settings.json so the agent cannot rewrite its own rules, pids/mem/cpu limits to stop fork bombs, and no Docker socket, SSH keys, or host credentials mounted.
quote: iptables default-deny policy blocks all outbound traffic except the Anthropic API and Docker-embedded DNS ... No Docker socket, no SSH keys, no host credentials mounted.

claim: Mounting /var/run/docker.sock is worse than --privileged because the container looks unprivileged to scanners yet can spawn unlimited new privileged containers; a one-liner like `docker run --privileged --pid=host -v /:/host ubuntu chroot /host bash` from inside the sandbox owns the host.
quote: docker run -it --rm --privileged --pid=host --net=host -v /:/host ubuntu:22.04 chroot /host bash ... The container looks completely normal (not privileged).

claim: The o1 system card and subsequent SandboxEscapeBench work from Oxford and the UK AI Security Institute show frontier LLMs can scan for and exploit exposed Docker APIs and misconfigurations from inside a sandbox, and recommend dropping CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_MODULE, CAP_DAC_READ_SEARCH, and CAP_NET_RAW when deploying AI agents.
quote: the model discovered a misconfigured Docker API on the evaluation host, used network scanning to identify the exposed daemon, and exploited it to spawn containers and read protected files.

claim: Wiz's 2025 IMDS research shows attackers routinely pivot from SSRF or compromised workloads to 169.254.169.254 to steal temporary IAM credentials, and recommends blocking that endpoint (and IPv6 fd00:ec2::254) at the container network layer for any workload that doesn't need it.
quote: Track egress requests from workloads and APIs to private and internal IP ranges and cloud metadata IPs such as AWS 169.254.169.254 and IPv6 [fd00:ec2::254].

claim: The canonical AI-agent Docker hardening recipe combines --read-only (immutable rootfs), --tmpfs /tmp with noexec,nosuid, --security-opt=no-new-privileges, --cap-drop=ALL with targeted --cap-add only where needed, and --user 1000:1000 to avoid root in the container.
quote: --read-only (immutable filesystem prevents binary modification), --security-opt=no-new-privileges (blocks privilege escalation), --cap-drop=ALL (removes all Linux capabilities) ... running as a non-root user (1000:1000).

claim: For workloads where Docker namespace isolation feels insufficient, gVisor (user-space syscall interception), Kata Containers (lightweight VMs under a container API), and Firecracker microVMs (~100-200ms boot, KVM-enforced) are the three stronger-isolation options, with Firecracker considered the gold standard for running untrusted AI-generated code.
quote: Firecracker MicroVM's combination of hardware-level isolation plus millisecond startup plus extremely low overhead is widely recognized as the optimal gold standard for running untrusted AI code.

claim: For domain-level HTTPS allowlisting without MITM certificates, iron-proxy and Squid in SNI peek/splice mode inspect the TLS ClientHello and splice or reject based on an allowlist, which is the preferred pattern over mitmproxy (a debugger, not an enforcer) for production agent egress gateways.
quote: Iron-proxy is purpose-built for controlling and auditing egress from untrusted workloads. Mitmproxy is a great debugging tool but isn't designed for production enforcement.
